Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Transitive possessive verb phrase: ki-pia ‘s/he has’ + possessed noun i-yolo ‘his/her heart’; flexible clause idiom.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Someone is caring, generous, emotionally present, or morally brave when others need help.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Yeh kuali tlakatl wan kuali techpaleuia.
Usage Contexts: family help; community work; praise; caregiving; village leadership
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Built on the Nahuatl body–heart metaphor in which yolo marks feeling, courage, and moral intention; the form is especially useful in family and mutual-aid speech.
First Example Sentence: Non siwatl kipia iyolo, pampa nochipa kinpaleuia n kokoxkeh.
Second Example Sentence: Ma tiknemilikan kuali, akin kipia iyolo amo kinkahua iselti n tokniwan.
Third Example Sentence: Ich kalnechikolli okijtojkeh ke n telpokatl kipia iyolo wan kuali tekiti.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Intransitive emotional predicate: paki ‘rejoices’ + possessed subject-like noun i-yolo ‘his/her heart’; fixed metaphorical clause.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: The person feels deep happiness, relief, or grateful joy.
Usage Contexts: celebrations; family news; gratitude; reunions; school achievement
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Reflects the longstanding Nahuatl pattern of locating emotion in the heart; common for sincere joy after good news or safe return.
First Example Sentence: Paki iyolo n lamatsin keman okalakiko ikonetsi de escuela.
Second Example Sentence: Nopiltzin paki iyolo pampa otikchijkeh se kuali ilwitl.
Third Example Sentence: Keman okikakkeh ke ayakmo kokoxki, nochi kalijtik paki ininyolo.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Intransitive stative clause: kokowa ‘hurts/is sick’ + possessed noun i-yolo; body-part metaphor for emotional pain.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: The person is sad, worried, hurt, or grieving inside.
Usage Contexts: condolences; family conflict; migration; illness; intimate conversation
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Grounded in a regional heart-centered emotional vocabulary; appropriate when pain is moral or relational, not only physical.
First Example Sentence: Kokowa iyolo n nana pampa ipiltzin oyah wejka tekiti.
Second Example Sentence: Amo xiktlahtolkokoli; nikitta ke kokowa moyolo.
Third Example Sentence: Keman otikmatkeh tlen opanok, okokox toyolo wan otimokajkeh san titlakakih.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Locative existential clause: pronoun + kahki ‘is/stands’ + locative phrase itech ome ohtli ‘on two roads’; metaphorical spatial idiom.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Someone is undecided between two choices or pulled by conflicting obligations.
Usage Contexts: advice; work choices; marriage decisions; migration; politics
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Uses the road as a life-choice metaphor, resonant in mountain communities where paths connect homes, markets, fields, and migration routes.
First Example Sentence: Yeh kahki itech ome ohtli: kisas tekiti o mokawas kampa ichan.
Second Example Sentence: Amo xikchiwa san nima, pampa tikah itech ome ohtli wan moneki tiknemiliz.
Third Example Sentence: N tekiuajketl kahki itech ome ohtli keman n altepetl kitlahtlania atl wan tomin.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Reflexive/intransitive verb phrase: mo-kawa ‘s/he remains’ + instrumental ika + possessed noun i-tlajtol ‘his/her word’; idiom of commitment.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Someone keeps a promise, stands by what they said, or remains trustworthy.
Usage Contexts: agreements; workplace; civic meetings; family obligations; conflict mediation
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Speech is socially binding in Nahua communal life; this idiom frames a person’s word as a place where moral reliability remains.
First Example Sentence: Notata mokawa ika itlajtol; tla kijtoa ke hualas, hualas.
Second Example Sentence: Itech tekitl san akin mokawa ika itlajtol kuali motta.
Third Example Sentence: Okimakakeh tekitl pampa nochi kimatih ke yeh mokawa ika itlajtol.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Transitive verb phrase: ki-powa ‘s/he counts/reads’ + instrumental phrase ika ome ixtelolo ‘with two eyes’; fixed idiom of careful attention.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Someone examines something carefully, not superficially.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Yeh kitta kuali wan amo san panowa.
Usage Contexts: crafts; contracts; schoolwork; farming; medical instructions
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Connects seeing with responsibility; useful in settings where careful reading, counting, seed selection, or medicine use prevents harm.
First Example Sentence: Xikpowa ika ome ixtelolo n amatl antes de tiksignaroz.
Second Example Sentence: N tlamachtijketl techilwia ma tikpowakan ika ome toixtelolo tlen otijkuilojkeh.
Third Example Sentence: Keman tikkowa sintli, xikpowa ika ome ixtelolo pampa amo nochi kuali.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Intransitive motion/event clause: kisa ‘comes out’ + noun poctli ‘smoke’ + locative itech ichan ‘from/at his/her house’; metaphorical warning idiom.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: There is visible trouble, conflict, or a problem that can no longer be hidden.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Onka se kuesolli wan nochi ya kittah.
Usage Contexts: gossip; family conflict; political criticism; warnings; narrative
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Smoke is a household sign that something is burning or cooking; figuratively it marks hidden tension becoming visible in community life.
First Example Sentence: Kisa poctli itech ichan pampa nochi vecinos yokikakkeh n kuesolli.
Second Example Sentence: Amo xikijto ke amo tleno panowa; ya kisa poctli itech mochan.
Third Example Sentence: Itech municipio kisa poctli pampa n gente kitlahtlania tlen kanin oyah n tomin.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Nominal-adverbial unity phrase: san se yolo ‘only one heart’ + intransitive plural verb ti-tekiti ‘we work’; collective idiom.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: People act with unity, shared purpose, and mutual trust.
Usage Contexts: community work; family cooperation; festivals; school teams; assemblies
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Echoes tequio/faena-style collective labor and Nahua values of reciprocity; the heart represents shared intention.
First Example Sentence: San se yolo titekiti, yeka n ilwitl kisa kuali.
Second Example Sentence: Tla san se yolo titekiti, welis tikyektlaliskeh n atlajkolli.
Third Example Sentence: N siwameh wan n tlakameh san se yolo tekiti ipan kalnechikolli.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Negative transitive sensory-cognitive clause: amo + ki-kaki ‘s/he hears/listens to’ + i-yolo ‘his/her heart’; idiom of ignored conscience.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Someone ignores their conscience, better judgment, or inner feeling.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Yeh amo kimokuitlahuia tlen kuali kimachilia.
Usage Contexts: moral advice; family correction; political critique; storytelling; sarcasm
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Combines hearing with the heart as an inner moral voice; strong but not vulgar, often used in counsel or reproach.
First Example Sentence: Amo kikaki iyolo, yeka okichi tlen okinkokoh n tokniwan.
Second Example Sentence: Xikaki moyolo antes de tikijtos se tlahtolli tlen tekokowa.
Third Example Sentence: N tekiuajketl amo kikaki iyolo keman n altepetl okitlahtlani tlen melawak.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Transitive-body metaphor clause: ki-mama ‘s/he carries’ + i-tonal ‘his/her day/spirit/fortune’ + locative ipan i-ajkol ‘on his/her shoulder’; poetic idiom.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Someone carries heavy responsibility, fate, or emotional burden with patience.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Yeh kipia miak tekitl wan kuesolli, pero kiyolchikawa.
Usage Contexts: literature; elder speech; migration stories; leadership; grief and resilience
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Draws on the cultural weight of tonal as life force/day-sign and the shoulder as the body’s load-bearing place; suitable for high-register narrative.
First Example Sentence: Kimama itonal ipan iajkol n nana, pero amo keman kinkahua ipilwah.
Second Example Sentence: N tekitini kimama itonal ipan iajkol keman kisa sejsepa para wejka.
Third Example Sentence: Itech n kuento, n huehue tlakatl kimama itonal ipan iajkol wan ok techmachtia ma tiyolchikawakan.
